paragraphs.text: I appreciate the chance to comment on the Digital Fairness Act. I want to share a personal worry that, in my opinion, is an increasing trend among online shoppers, particularly in the gaming industry. As a longtime player and consumer of the video game industry, I am extremely concerned that even after spending money, time, and emotion on a game, I cannot be sure that I will be able to access or play it in the future. Publishers are increasingly reserving the right to block access to games, shut down servers, or stop support, frequently making games useless even for people who have legitimately bought them. This leads to an unsettling disparity between copyright holders and consumers. If a game is going to be discontinued, it should, in what I believe, at least still be playable and functional. Customers' trust in digital services is damaged when a product is completely removed without providing alternatives or compensation. This issue has been aptly brought to light by initiatives like Stop Killing Games: we are merely requesting the right to continue using the content we have already paid for, not ongoing updates or support. We demand protections against businesses removing games from paying users without cause. A significant chance to solve these issues and establish more robust safeguards for digital ownership and long-term access is provided by the Digital Fairness Act. I implore you to take into account the rights of customers in this area not only as users, but also as legitimate proprietors of the goods they buy. I appreciate you taking the time to read this feedback.
